nodes.text: Good evening. Let's take a moment to reflect on your day.
When something didn't go as planned today, what was your first reaction?
options: I thought about what I could have done differently
I felt frustrated by things outside my control
I adapted and found another way
I waited for someone else to fix it

if: I thought about what I could have done differently
I adapted and found another way
I felt frustrated by things outside my control
I waited for someone else to fix it

nodes.text: You stepped up when things changed. What drove that response?
options: I felt responsible for the outcome
I realized complaining wouldn't help
I wanted to keep momentum going
It just felt like the only option

nodes.text: It’s draining when things feel out of our hands. How did you handle that feeling?
options: I eventually looked for what I could control
I voiced my frustration to others
I disengaged for a bit
I let circumstances dictate my next steps

if: I felt responsible for the outcome
I wanted to keep momentum going
I realized complaining wouldn't help
It just felt like the only option
I eventually looked for what I could control
I voiced my frustration to others
I disengaged for a bit
I let circumstances dictate my next steps

nodes.text: You see your hand in what happened today. That's agency — not everything went your way, but you stayed in the driver's seat.
Your agency was practical today. You might not have loved the situation, but you took charge of your response.
Even though you felt stuck initially, you searched for control. That shift is the foundation of resilience.
A tough day pulls attention outward — to what others did or didn't do. Fair enough. But finding even a sliver of choice can reduce that stress tomorrow.
Now let’s shift from how you handled things — to what you contributed.
Think about an interaction you had today. Which statement feels most accurate?
options: I stepped in because someone needed help
I noticed something broken and fixed it
I did my job, but felt underappreciated
I felt frustrated covering for others

if: I stepped in because someone needed help
I noticed something broken and fixed it
I did my job, but felt underappreciated
I felt frustrated covering for others

nodes.text: When you went beyond your core tasks, what was your focus?
options: Solving the problem
Supporting the team's goals
Making sure I got credit later
Just getting it off my plate

nodes.text: When we feel underappreciated or heavily burdened, our focus narrows. Today, were you focused on what you lacked or what you gave?
options: I was focused on the recognition I wasn't getting
I felt resentful about the workload gap
I recognized my frustration but tried to help anyway
I emotionally checked out to protect my energy

if: Solving the problem
Supporting the team's goals
Making sure I got credit later
Just getting it off my plate
I recognized my frustration but tried to help anyway
I was focused on the recognition I wasn't getting
I felt resentful about the workload gap
I emotionally checked out to protect my energy

nodes.text: You focused on what you could give rather than what you were owed. Contribution builds value and trust over time.
It’s completely normal to feel frustrated when things are unbalanced. The fact that you still contributed speaks volumes.
You stepped up, but your energy was tied to getting credit or escaping the task. When we tether our effort purely to transactional rewards, we court burnout.
It’s natural to expect fairness—but waiting for others to 'make it right' limits your own impact. True growth often starts by shifting focus back to what you can offer.
Let’s expand the lens one final time — beyond the tasks, to the people around you.
Think about the most stressful moment today. Who were you optimizing for?
options: Myself — I just needed to survive the day
My immediate team — we needed to hit our goal
A specific colleague — they needed my support
The customer/end-user — delivering value to them

if: Myself — I just needed to survive the day
My immediate team — we needed to hit our goal
A specific colleague — they needed my support
The customer/end-user — delivering value to them

nodes.text: Survival mode narrows our vision. You carried the day entirely on your own shoulders. Expanding your radius of concern next time might actually reduce the pressure.
You saw the shared nature of the challenge. That awareness strengthens collaboration and distributes the emotional load.
You set aside your own priorities to imagine another person’s experience. That shift toward empathy is a hallmark of self-transcendence.
You connected your immediate work to a larger purpose. Focusing outward is where meaning deepens and burnout loses its grip.

Today you felt that '{A1_OPEN.answer}'. By maintaining a(n) {axis1.dominant} locus of control and a(n) {axis2.dominant} orientation, you directly shape your environment. In the heat of the moment, your focus ended up {axis3.dominant}-centric. Awareness is the first step toward intentional change.
See you tomorrow.
